text.tspan: The April 2025 tariff announcements added a second
layer of complexity. While original artworks were
officially exempt from the new reciprocal tariffs (a White
House fact sheet confirmed that artworks, photographs,
and posters were excluded), the broader trade war
rattled confidence and hammered the dollar
simultaneously.
Impacted by international tariffs and
shipping costs, per the Art Basel report.
Market shifting toward domestic trading.
Collectors cancelled US-bound purchases, galleries
postponed American projects, and auction houses
reshuffled sales calendars. The uncertainty was as
damaging as any actual tariff. Even with the art
exemption, buyers worried about customs enforcement,
classification disputes, and future policy changes.
The dollar's tariff-driven fall had a paradoxical effect
on the auction market. It made US sales cheaper for
foreign buyers, but the policy chaos deterred some of
those same buyers from participating. Currency
weakness invited foreign capital in theory; trade
uncertainty pushed it away in practice.
